content:
# Genesis Chapter 26 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 14v–15r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_026_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_026_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 22–23 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0026

---

*Famine — The LORD's Command to Remain in the Land (vv. 1–6)*

**26:1** And there was [וַיְהִי] a famine [רָעָב] in the land [בָּאָרֶץ] — besides [מִלְּבַד] the first famine [הָרָעָב הָרִאשׁוֹן] that was [אֲשֶׁר הָיָה] in the days of [בִּימֵי] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] — and Isaac went [וַיֵּלֶךְ יִצְחָק] to Abimelech [אֶל-אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] king [מֶלֶךְ] of the Philistines [פְּלִשְׁתִּים] — to Gerar [גְּרָרָה].

**26:2** And the LORD appeared [וַיֵּרָא אֵלָיו יְהוָה] to him and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Do not go down [אַל-תֵּרֵד] to Egypt [מִצְרָיְמָה] — dwell [שְׁכֹן] in the land [בָּאָרֶץ] that I will tell you [אֲשֶׁר אֹמַר אֵלֶיךָ].

**26:3** Sojourn [גּוּר] in this land [בָּאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת] and I will be with you [וְאֶהְיֶה עִמְּךָ] and I will bless you [וַאֲבָרֶכְךָ] — for to you [כִּי-לְךָ] and to your seed [וּלְזַרְעֲךָ] I will give [אֶתֵּן] all these [אֶת-כָּל-הָאֲרָצֹת] lands [הָאֵלֶּה] and I will establish [וַהֲקִמֹתִי] the oath [אֶת-הַשְּׁבֻעָה] that I swore [אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי] to Abraham [לְאַבְרָהָם] your father [אָבִיךָ].

**26:4** And I will multiply [וְהִרְבֵּיתִי] your seed [אֶת-זַרְעֲךָ] as the stars of [כְּכוֹכְבֵי] the heavens [הַשָּׁמַיִם] and I will give [וְנָתַתִּי] to your seed [לְזַרְעֲךָ] all these [אֵת כָּל-הָאֲרָצֹת] lands [הָאֵלֶּה] — and all the nations of the earth [וְהִתְבָּרְכוּ בְזַרְעֲךָ כֹּל גּוֹיֵי הָאָרֶץ] shall bless themselves [*hitbarekhu*] through your seed [בְּזַרְעֲךָ].

**26:5** Because [עֵקֶב] Abraham [אֲשֶׁר-שָׁמַע אַבְרָהָם] hearkened to my voice [בְּקֹלִי] and kept [וַיִּשְׁמֹר] my charge [מִשְׁמַרְתִּי] — my commandments [מִצְוֺתַי] — my statutes [חֻקּוֹתַי] — and my laws [וְתוֹרֹתָי].

**26:6** And Isaac dwelt [וַיֵּשֶׁב יִצְחָק] in Gerar [בִּגְרָר].

---

*The Wife-Sister Deception — Rebekah at Gerar (vv. 7–11)*

**26:7** And the men of [וַיִּשְׁאֲלוּ אַנְשֵׁי] the place [הַמָּקוֹם] asked [שָׁאַל] about his wife [לְאִשְׁתּוֹ] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: She is my sister [אֲחֹתִי הִוא] — for [כִּי] he feared [יָרֵא] to say [לֵאמֹר] [she is] my wife [אִשְׁתִּי] — [thinking] lest [פֶּן] the men of the place [אַנְשֵׁי הַמָּקוֹם] kill me [יַהַרְגֻנִי] on account of [עַל-דְּבַר] Rebekah [רִבְקָה] — because [כִּי] she was beautiful [טוֹבַת מַרְאֶה] of appearance [הִוא].

**26:8** And it happened [וַיְהִי] — when his days there were long [כִּי אָרְכוּ-לוֹ שָׁם הַיָּמִים] — that Abimelech [וַיַּשְׁקֵף אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] king of the Philistines [מֶלֶךְ פְּלִשְׁתִּים] looked out [שָׁקַף] through the window [בְּעַד הַחַלּוֹן] and he saw [וַיַּרְא] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] — Isaac [יִצְחָק] was caressing [מְצַחֵק] Rebekah [אֵת רִבְקָה] his wife [אִשְׁתּוֹ] [*metsacheq — see note*].

**26:9** And Abimelech called [וַיִּקְרָא אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] to Isaac [לְיִצְחָק] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Yet indeed [אַךְ הִנֵּה] she is your wife [אִשְׁתְּךָ הִוא] — and how [וְאֵיךְ] did you say [אָמַרְתָּ] she is my sister [אֲחֹתִי הִוא]? And Isaac said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו יִצְחָק] to him: Because [כִּי] I said [אָמַרְתִּי] lest [פֶּן] I die [אָמוּת] on account of her [עָלֶיהָ].

**26:10** And Abimelech said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ]: What [מַה] is this [זֹּאת] you have done [עָשִׂיתָ] to us [לָּנוּ]? As a little thing [כִּמְעַט] one of the people [שָׁכַב אַחַד הָעָם] might have lain [שָׁכַב] with your wife [אֶת-אִשְׁתֶּךָ] and you would have brought [וְהֵבֵאתָ] guilt [אָשָׁם] upon us.

**26:11** And Abimelech commanded [וַיְצַו אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] all the people [אֶת-כָּל-הָעָם] saying [לֵאמֹר]: He who touches [הַנֹּגֵעַ] this man [בָּאִישׁ הַזֶּה] or his wife [וּבְאִשְׁתּוֹ] — dying he shall be put to death [מוֹת יוּמָת].

---

*Isaac Prospers — Philistine Envy — Departure (vv. 12–16)*

**26:12** And Isaac sowed [וַיִּזְרַע יִצְחָק] in that land [בָּאָרֶץ הַהִוא] and found [וַיִּמְצָא] in that year [בַּשָּׁנָה הַהִוא] a hundredfold [מֵאָה שְׁעָרִים] — and the LORD blessed him [וַיְבָרְכֵהוּ יְהוָה].

**26:13** And the man [וַיִּגְדַּל הָאִישׁ] became great [גָּדַל] and he went on [וַיֵּלֶךְ] and became great [גָּדוֹל] until [עַד] he became very great [כִּי-גָדַל מְאֹד].

**26:14** And he had [וַיְהִי-לוֹ] possessions of sheep [מִקְנֵה-צֹאן] and possessions of cattle [וּמִקְנֵה בָקָר] and much [רַב] service [עֲבֻדָּה] — and the Philistines [וַיְקַנְאוּ אֹתוֹ פְּלִשְׁתִּים] envied him.

**26:15** And all the wells [וְכָל-הַבְּאֵרֹת] that the servants of his father [אֲשֶׁר חָפְרוּ עַבְדֵי אָבִיו] had dug in the days of [בִּימֵי] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] his father [אָבִיו] — the Philistines [פְּלִשְׁתִּים] had stopped them up [סְתָמוּם] and filled them [וַיְמַלְאוּם] with earth [עָפָר].

**26:16** And Abimelech said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] to Isaac [אֶל-יִצְחָק]: Go from us [לֵךְ מֵעִמָּנוּ] — for [כִּי] you have become far mightier [עָצַמְתָּ מִמֶּנּוּ] than us [מְאֹד] — [very much].

---

*The Re-Digging of Abraham's Wells — Esek, Sitnah, Rehoboth (vv. 17–22)*

**26:17** And Isaac went from there [וַיֵּלֶךְ מִשָּׁם יִצְחָק] and encamped [וַיִּחַן] in the valley of [בְּנַחַל] Gerar [גְּרָר] and dwelt [וַיֵּשֶׁב] there [שָׁם].

**26:18** And Isaac returned [וַיָּשָׁב יִצְחָק] and dug [וַיַּחְפֹּר] the wells of water [אֶת-בְּאֵרֹת הַמַּיִם] that they had dug [אֲשֶׁר חָפְרוּ] in the days of [בִּימֵי] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] his father [אָבִיו] — that the Philistines had stopped up [וַיְסַתְּמוּם פְּלִשְׁתִּים] after the death of [אַחֲרֵי מוֹת] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] — and he called [וַיִּקְרָא] to them [לָהֶם] names [שֵׁמוֹת] according to the names [כַּשֵּׁמֹת] that his father [אֲשֶׁר-קָרָא לָהֶן אָבִיו] had called them.

**26:19** And the servants of Isaac [וַיַּחְפְּרוּ עַבְדֵי-יִצְחָק] dug in the valley [בַּנָּחַל] and found there [וַיִּמְצְאוּ-שָׁם] a well of [בְּאֵר] living water [מַיִם חַיִּים].

**26:20** And the shepherds of Gerar [וַיָּרִיבוּ רֹעֵי גְרָר] quarreled [רִיב] with the shepherds of Isaac [עִם-רֹעֵי יִצְחָק] saying [לֵאמֹר]: The water is ours [לָנוּ הַמָּיִם] — and he called [וַיִּקְרָא] the name of the well [שֵׁם-הַבְּאֵר] Esek [עֵשֶׂק] [*contention*] — because [כִּי] they contended [הִתְעַשְּׂקוּ] with him.

**26:21** And they dug [וַיַּחְפְּרוּ] another well [בְּאֵר אַחֶרֶת] and they quarreled [וַיָּרִיבוּ] over it [גַּם-עָלֶיהָ] also — and he called [וַיִּקְרָא] its name [שְׁמָהּ] Sitnah [שִׂטְנָה] [*hostility / accusation*].

**26:22** And he removed [וַיֶּעְתֵּק] from there [מִשָּׁם] and dug [וַיַּחְפֹּר] another well [בְּאֵר אַחֶרֶת] — and they did not quarrel [וְלֹא-רָבוּ] over it — and he called [וַיִּקְרָא] its name [שְׁמָהּ] Rehoboth [רְחֹבוֹת] [*broad places / room*] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: For now [כִּי-עַתָּה] the LORD has made room [הִרְחִיב יְהוָה] for us [לָנוּ] and we shall be fruitful [וּפָרִינוּ] in the land [בָּאָרֶץ].

---

*The LORD Appears at Beer-sheba — The Covenant Renewed (vv. 23–25)*

**26:23** And he went up [וַיַּעַל] from there [מִשָּׁם] to Beer-sheba [בְּאֵר שָׁבַע].

**26:24** And the LORD appeared [וַיֵּרָא אֵלָיו יְהוָה] to him on that night [בַּלַּיְלָה הַהוּא] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I am the God [אָנֹכִי אֱלֹהֵי] of Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] your father [אָבִיךָ] — do not fear [אַל-תִּירָא] — for I am with you [כִּי-אִתְּךָ אָנֹכִי] — and I will bless you [וּבֵרַכְתִּיךָ] and I will multiply [וְהִרְבֵּיתִי] your seed [אֶת-זַרְעֲךָ] for the sake of [בַּעֲבוּר] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] my servant [עַבְדִּי].

**26:25** And he built [וַיִּבֶן] there [שָׁם] an altar [מִזְבֵּחַ] and called [וַיִּקְרָא] on the name of [בְּשֵׁם] the LORD [יְהוָה] and he pitched [וַיֶּט] his tent [אָהֳלוֹ] there [שָׁם] — and the servants of Isaac [עַבְדֵי-יִצְחָק] dug [וַיִּכְרוּ] a well [בְּאֵר] there.

---

*Abimelech Comes — The Covenant with Isaac (vv. 26–33)*

**26:26** And Abimelech went [וַאֲבִימֶלֶךְ הָלַךְ] to him from Gerar [אֵלָיו מִגְּרָר] — and Ahuzzath [וַאֲחֻזַּת] his companion [מֵרֵעֵהוּ] and Phicol [וּפִיכֹל] the commander of [שַׂר] his army [צְבָאוֹ].

**26:27** And Isaac said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יִצְחָק] to them: Why [מַדּוּעַ] have you come to me [בָּאתֶם אֵלָי] seeing that [וְאַתֶּם] you hate me [שְׂנֵאתֶם אֹתִי] and sent me away [וַתְּשַׁלְּחוּנִי] from you [מֵאִתְּכֶם]?

**26:28** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: We have seen plainly [רָאוֹ רָאִינוּ] that [כִּי] the LORD [יְהוָה] has been with you [הָיָה עִמָּךְ] — and we said [וַנֹּאמֶר]: Let there be now [תְּהִי נָא] an oath [אָלָה] between us [בֵּינוֹתֵינוּ] — between us [בֵּינֵינוּ] and between you [וּבֵינֶךָ] — and let us cut [וְנִכְרְתָה] a covenant [בְּרִית] with you [עִמָּךְ].

**26:29** [Swear] that you will not do [אִם-תַּעֲשֵׂה] with us [עִמָּנוּ] evil [רָעָה] — just as [כַּאֲשֶׁר] we did not touch you [לֹא-נְגַעֲנוּךָ] and just as [וְכַאֲשֶׁר] we did to you [עָשִׂינוּ עִמְּךָ] only good [רַק-טוֹב] and sent you away [וַנְּשַׁלֵּחֲךָ] in peace [בְּשָׁלוֹם] — you [אַתָּה] now are [עַתָּה] the blessed of [בְּרוּךְ] the LORD [יְהוָה].

**26:30** And he made [וַיַּעַשׂ] for them [לָהֶם] a feast [מִשְׁתֶּה] and they ate [וַיֹּאכְלוּ] and they drank [וַיִּשְׁתּוּ].

**26:31** And they rose early [וַיַּשְׁכִּימוּ] in the morning [בַּבֹּקֶר] and swore [וַיִּשָּׁבְעוּ] each man [אִישׁ] to his brother [לְאָחִיו] — and Isaac sent them away [וַיְשַׁלְּחֵם יִצְחָק] and they went from him [וַיֵּלְכוּ מֵאִתּוֹ] in peace [בְּשָׁלוֹם].

**26:32** And it happened [וַיְהִי] on that day [בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא] that the servants of Isaac [וַיָּבֹאוּ עַבְדֵי יִצְחָק] came and told him [וַיַּגִּידוּ לוֹ] about the well [עַל-אֹדוֹת הַבְּאֵר] that they had dug [אֲשֶׁר חָפָרוּ] — and they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ] to him: We have found [מָצָאנוּ] water [מָיִם].

**26:33** And he called it [וַיִּקְרָא אֹתָהּ] Shibah [שִׁבְעָה] [*oath / seven*] — therefore [עַל-כֵּן] the name of [שֵׁם-הָעִיר] the city [הָעִיר] is Beer-sheba [בְּאֵר שָׁבַע] until this day [עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה].

---

*Esau's Hittite Wives — Bitterness to Isaac and Rebekah (vv. 34–35)*

**26:34** And Esau [וַיְהִי עֵשָׂו] was forty [בֶּן-אַרְבָּעִים] years old [שָׁנָה] and he took [וַיִּקַּח] Judith [אֶת-יְהוּדִית] the daughter of [בַּת-] Beeri [בְּאֵרִי] the Hittite [הַחִתִּי] and Basemath [וְאֶת-בָּשְׂמַת] the daughter of [בַּת-] Elon [אֵילֹן] the Hittite [הַחִתִּי].

**26:35** And they were [וַתִּהְיֶיןָ] a bitterness of spirit [מֹרַת רוּחַ] to Isaac [לְיִצְחָק] and to Rebekah [וּלְרִבְקָה].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On reading the Leningrad Codex, Folios 14v–15r:**
Page 1 (folio 14v) of the Genesis 26 source images is the inverted verso of the leaf whose recto (folio 14r) carries Genesis 24, and whose verso (folio 14v) carries the transition from Genesis 25 into Genesis 26. The image as photographed is rotated — once properly oriented, three clearly ruled columns of Masoretic Hebrew are visible. The right column and center column carry the end of Genesis 25 and the beginning of Genesis 26 (vv. 1 through approximately v. 22). The Masoretic punctuation, vowel pointing (*nikud*), and cantillation marks (*te'amim*) are well preserved and legible throughout. A *parashah petuchah* (open section, פ) marker is visible before Genesis 26:1, correctly demarcating the new narrative unit. A second *setumah* (closed section, ס) marker appears before v. 12, and another before v. 17, correctly dividing the chapter's narrative movements.

Page 2 (folio 15r) presents in standard orientation with the folio number visible. It carries the text from approximately v. 23 through the end of chapter 26 and into the opening verses of Genesis 27. The script is clear and the three-column layout is consistent with folio 14v. The *parashah* markers for the covenant scene (v. 23) and the close of the chapter are visible.

Codex Vaticanus pages 22–23 carry the LXX text of Genesis 26 in continuous three-column uncial. Page 22 (online viewer 0026) carries Genesis 26 from v. 1 through approximately v. 22, with the marginal notation *CAP. XXXIX* visible at the left margin marking the ancient Greek chapter division. Page 23 (online viewer 0027) continues from approximately v. 23 through the end of chapter 26 and into Genesis 27, with *CAP. XLV* visible at the left margin.

---

**26:1 — "Besides the first famine that was in the days of Abraham" (*milevad hara'av harishon asher hayah bimei Avraham*, מִלְּבַד הָרָעָב הָרִאשׁוֹן אֲשֶׁר הָיָה בִּימֵי אַבְרָהָם):**
The narrator inserts a rare explicit retrospective comparison, linking this famine to the one that drove Abraham to Egypt (Gen 12:10). The phrase *milevad* ("besides, apart from, in addition to") clarifies: this is a *second* famine, not the original one. The comparison implicitly invites the reader to track how Isaac's behavior in a parallel situation will parallel or diverge from Abraham's. Both men are driven by famine toward foreign kings; both deceive about their wives; both emerge enriched. Yet Isaac is explicitly commanded *not* to go to Egypt (v. 2), whereas Abraham went without such direction. The narrator's gloss flags the pattern for the attentive reader. The LXX Vaticanus reads *chōris tou protou limou* (χωρὶς τοῦ πρώτου λιμοῦ) — "apart from/besides the first famine" — a precise equivalent of *milevad*.

---

**26:3 — "I will be with you" (*we'ehyeh 'immekha*, וְאֶהְיֶה עִמָּךְ) — The *'ehyeh* Echo:**
The divine promise in v. 3 opens with *we'ehyeh 'immekha* — "and I will be with you" — using the first-person imperfect form *'ehyeh* (אֶהְיֶה), the same form that will become the great divine self-disclosure to Moses at the burning bush: *'ehyeh 'asher 'ehyeh* ("I am/will be what I am/will be," Exod 3:14). In context here, *'ehyeh* is a simple promise of divine presence and accompaniment — the same formula given to the patriarchs (cf. 28:15 to Jacob; 31:3; 46:4) — but for readers of the completed Torah, the word vibrates with its Sinai resonance. The LORD who promises to *be with* Isaac in a time of famine is the same LORD who, centuries later, names himself as the one who simply and absolutely *is*. EMET preserves *'ehyeh* in transliteration and renders it literally as "I will be with you" without domesticating the form.

---

**26:4 — "All the nations of the earth shall bless themselves" (*hitbarekhu*, הִתְבָּרְכוּ) — The Reflexive Blessing Formula:**
The verb *hitbarekhu* (הִתְבָּרְכוּ, Hitpael of *barak*, "to bless") is here, as at 22:18 and 12:3, a reflexive or tolerative form: the nations *bless themselves* — they invoke the name of Abraham's seed as a formula of blessing for themselves, saying in effect "may we be like you." The Hitpael is distinct from the Niphal *nibrekhu* at 12:3 and 18:18 ("they shall be blessed") — the Hitpael specifically emphasizes the active, self-directed nature of the blessing invocation. EMET renders it "shall bless themselves" and preserves *hitbarekhu* in transliteration as a key theological term. The LXX Vaticanus reads *eneulogēthēsontai en tō spermati sou* (ἐνευλογηθήσονται ἐν τῷ σπέρματί σου) — "they will be blessed in your seed" — using the passive form, which matches the Niphal reading pattern rather than the Hitpael's reflexive nuance. This is a theologically notable difference: the LXX makes the blessing passive (received), while the MT makes it reflexive (self-invoked). The distinction bears on whether the promise is universal (nations passively receive blessing) or instrumental (nations actively invoke Israel's God as their blessing-formula).

---

**26:8 — *Metsacheq* (מְצַחֵק) — Isaac "Laughing/Caressing" Rebekah:**
The word Abimelech sees — *metsacheq* (מְצַחֵק, Piel participle of *tsachaq*, "to laugh, to play, to sport, to caress") — is a verbal form of the very root from which Isaac's name derives: *Yitschaq* (יִצְחָק, "he laughs"). The irony is dense and deliberate. Abimelech observes *Yitschaq* (*the-laughing-one*) engaged in *metsacheq* (laughing/playing/caressing). The verb in the Piel has a range of meanings: to laugh (Gen 21:6), to play or sport (Exod 32:6 — the golden calf incident), to caress or make love (here and possibly 39:14 in the Potiphar's wife episode). The context makes clear that whatever Abimelech saw was intimate enough to reveal the marital relationship — yet public enough for a king to see through a window. The most likely sense is amorous or erotic play between a married couple, not visible from a sister. EMET renders it "was caressing" with a note, but preserves *metsacheq* in transliteration to honor the wordplay with Isaac's name that no English translation can fully reproduce: Isaac was *Isaac-ing* Rebekah. The LXX Vaticanus reads *paizonta meta Rebekkas tēs gynaikos autou* (παίζοντα μετὰ Ρεβέκκας τῆς γυναικὸς αὐτοῦ) — "playing/sporting with Rebekah his wife" — *paizō* ("to play, to sport") preserves the ambiguity of playful/erotic interaction but loses the specific *tsachaq*-root wordplay with the name Yitschaq.

---

**26:12 — "A hundredfold" (*me'ah she'arim*, מֵאָה שְׁעָרִים):**
The phrase is literally "a hundred gates" or "a hundred measures" — *she'arim* (שְׁעָרִים) from the root *ša'ar* (שַׁעַר, "gate, measure"), here denoting a unit of grain-measure. "A hundredfold return" in agricultural terms is an exceptional yield; normally four-to-tenfold would be a good harvest. The text explicitly attributes this extraordinary yield to the LORD's blessing (*waybarkhehu YHWH*). The LXX Vaticanus reads *hekatosteuousan krithēn* (ἑκατοστεύουσαν κριθήν) — "barley producing a hundredfold" — specifying the grain as barley, which is not in the MT. This is a significant LXX addition or variant, either reflecting a different Hebrew Vorlage or an interpretive specification by the Greek translator.

---

**26:13 — The Triple Crescendo of Greatness (*wayyigdal... wayyēlekh gadōl... kî-gadal mĕ'ōd*):**
The narrator deploys a syntactic crescendo: *wayyigdal ha'ish wayyēlekh gadōl 'ad kî-gadal mĕ'ōd* — literally "and the man became great and he walked great until that he became great very." The verb *gadal* (גָּדַל, "to grow, to become great") appears three times in a single verse: first as a finite verb (*wayyigdal*), then as an infinitive absolute (*gadōl*), and finally in the phrase *kî-gadal mĕ'ōd* ("for/that he became very great"). The accumulation enacts the growth it describes — the sentence keeps growing. EMET preserves the progression "became great... became great... became very great" to honor the verbal repetition.

---

**26:18 — Re-digging the Wells and Restoring Their Names:**
Isaac's act of re-digging Abraham's wells and restoring their original names (*kashshēmōt 'asher qārā' lāhen 'āvîv* — "according to the names that his father had called them") is a significant act of identity and continuity. Wells in the ancient Near East were not merely practical — they were property markers, covenant sites, and named places holding memory. The Philistines had "stopped them up" (*sĕtāmûm*, סְתָמוּם — from *sātam*, "to stop, to block, to seal off") specifically to erase Abraham's claims. Isaac's act reverses this erasure. He does not rename the wells with new names — he restores *the names his father gave them*. This is an act of filial piety and covenantal continuity: Isaac's identity is rooted in Abraham's legacy. The LXX Vaticanus reads *epekaleseto ep'autais onomata* (ἐπεκαλέσατο ἐπ'αὐταῖς ὀνόματα) — "he called upon them names" — a loose rendering that does not explicitly note the restoration of the *father's* names, losing the specific continuity emphasis of the MT.

---

**26:20–22 — The Well-Naming Theology: Esek, Sitnah, Rehoboth:**
The three well-names form a theological progression:

- **Esek** (עֵשֶׂק, v. 20) — from the root *'āsaq* (עָסַק, "to contend, to engage in dispute, to press upon"): *contention*. The herdsmen quarreled (*wayyārîvû*) over this well; its name memorializes the dispute.
- **Sitnah** (שִׂטְנָה, v. 21) — from the root *śāṭan* (שָׂטַן, "to accuse, to be an adversary, to oppose"): *hostility / accusation*. The same root gives us *śāṭān* (the adversary/satan). The name escalates: where Esek was "contention," Sitnah is adversarial opposition — the herdsmen are not merely competing, they are acting as enemies. The LXX renders Sitnah as *Echthran* (Ἔχθραν), "enmity/hostility" — a precise equivalent.
- **Rehoboth** (רְחֹבוֹת, v. 22) — from the root *rāḥav* (רָחַב, "to be broad, to have room, to expand"): *broad places / room*. Isaac's declaration makes the theology explicit: *kî-'attāh hirḥîv YHWH lānu ûpārînû vā'āreṣ* — "for now the LORD has made room for us and we shall be fruitful in the land." The movement from Esek to Sitnah to Rehoboth traces a journey from conflict through intensified opposition to divinely-given space and fruitfulness. EMET preserves all three names in transliteration with English glosses, as the Hebrew names are themselves the theological content.

---

**26:24 — "Do not fear, for I am with you" (*al-tîrā' kî-'ittĕkha 'ānōkhî*):**
The divine reassurance at Beer-sheba on the first night after Isaac's arrival uses the 'ānōkhî ("I") — the emphatic first-person pronoun — as its anchor: *kî-'ittĕkha 'ānōkhî* — "for with you, I" — placing the emphatic pronoun at the sentence's end for rhetorical stress. This is the divine self-presentation formula typical of patriarchal encounters: the LORD identifies himself ("I am the God of Abraham your father"), gives the prohibition of fear (*al-tîrā'*), grounds the prohibition in divine presence, and renews the covenant promise. The same structure appears at 28:13–15 (to Jacob at Bethel) and 46:3 (to Jacob descending to Egypt). The phrase *kî-'ittĕkha 'ānōkhî* — "for I am with you" — is the covenant formula in its most compressed form.

---

**26:28 — "Let there be an oath between us" (*tĕhî nā' 'ālāh bênōtênû*):**
Abimelech's proposal uses the word *'ālāh* (אָלָה, "oath, curse, sworn agreement") — not the more common *šĕvû'āh* ("oath"). The *'ālāh* carries both the positive sense of sworn commitment and the implicit negative sense of the curse that will fall on the oath-breaker. "Let there be a sworn curse between us" would be a more literal rendering. The dual sense is important: the Philistines are not merely asking for a friendly agreement but for a binding, self-imprecatory commitment. The verb *nikrĕtāh bĕrît* — "let us cut a covenant" — uses the idiom of covenant *cutting* (*kārat*, "to cut"), referencing the ancient practice of cutting animals to formalize covenants (cf. Gen 15). EMET preserves "let us cut a covenant" literally.

---

**26:33 — Shibah and Beer-sheba — Oath/Seven Etymology:**
The name *Šiḇ'āh* (שִׁבְעָה, "oath" or "seven") recapitulates the etiology of Beer-sheba already established at Gen 21:31 (where Abraham and Abimelech's covenant over the seven ewe-lambs provided the original etymology). Here Isaac re-encounters the same place with a new well-finding and gives it — again — a name built on the root *šb'* (ש-ב-ע, meaning both "seven" and "to swear"). The word *šĕvû'āh* ("oath") and the number *ševa'* ("seven") are from the same root. Beer-sheba (בְּאֵר שָׁבַע) thus means simultaneously "Well of the Oath" and "Well of Seven." That Isaac re-etymologizes a place already named by Abraham intensifies the theme of the chapter: Isaac is the faithful continuer, not an originator. He re-digs, renames, and re-establishes. The place gets its name confirmed again — etiology doubled, covenant anchored twice.

---

**26:34–35 — Esau's Wives as "Bitterness of Spirit" (*morat rûaḥ*, מֹרַת רוּחַ):**
The closing two verses function as a narrative bridge to Genesis 27, where Rebekah's fear of Canaanite daughters-in-law motivates her to send Jacob to Paddan-aram (Gen 27:46; 28:1). The phrase *mōrat rûaḥ* (מֹרַת רוּחַ) — "bitterness of spirit" — is a construct noun phrase: *mōrat* is a feminine construct form of *mārar* (מָרַר, "to be bitter"), and *rûaḥ* is "spirit, breath, inner being." The phrase thus means "bitterness-of-inner-being" — something that sits in the core of the person as an ongoing anguish rather than a momentary grief. Isaac and Rebekah are not merely displeased by Esau's marriages; the narrator says these women were a *constituted bitterness* to them. The choice of *rûaḥ* ("spirit/inner self") rather than, say, *lēv* ("heart") emphasizes the depth and persistence of the grief. The LXX Vaticanus reads *erithousan tō Isaak kai tē Rebekka* (ἐριθοῦσαν τῷ Ισαακ καὶ τῇ Ρεβέκκᾳ) — "contentious/troublesome to Isaac and Rebekah" — using *eritheuō* ("to be contentious, quarrelsome"), which captures the adversarial quality but loses the specific imagery of *mōrat rûaḥ* ("bitterness of spirit").

---

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 22–23):**
Codex Vaticanus pages 22–23 carry Genesis 26 across two pages in continuous three-column uncial. Key LXX variants and readings:

1. **26:1** — LXX *chōris tou protou limou* — "besides the first famine" — precise equivalent of MT *milevad hara'av harishon*.

2. **26:4** — LXX *eneulogēthēsontai* (ἐνευλογηθήσονται) — "they will be blessed" — Passive, rendering the MT *hitbarekhu* (Hitpael reflexive) as a passive. Significant theological variant: MT = nations actively bless themselves invoking your seed; LXX = nations passively receive blessing through your seed.

3. **26:8** — LXX *paizonta meta Rebekkas tēs gynaikos autou* — "playing/sporting with Rebekah his wife" — renders *metsacheq* as *paizō*, losing the *tsachaq*-wordplay with the name Yitschaq (Isaac).

4. **26:12** — LXX adds "barley" (*krithēn*, κριθήν) to the specification of the hundredfold crop. Not present in MT. Either reflects a variant Hebrew Vorlage or is an interpretive addition by the Greek translator.

5. **26:20** — LXX renders Esek as *Adikia* (Ἀδικία, "injustice/wrongdoing") rather than a transliteration, interpreting the well-name rather than preserving it. MT *Esek* = "contention."

6. **26:21** — LXX renders Sitnah as *Echthran* (Ἔχθραν, "hostility/enmity") — an accurate functional equivalent of *śāṭān*-root hostility.

7. **26:22** — LXX renders Rehoboth as *Eurychōria* (Εὐρυχωρία, "broad space, spaciousness") — an accurate equivalent of *rĕḥōvōt* from the root *rāḥav* ("to be broad").

8. **26:28** — LXX *genēsthō ara* (γενέσθω ἀρά) — "let there be a curse/oath [between us]" — *ara* (ἀρά) means "curse" or "imprecation," which matches the *'ālāh* (אָלָה) sense of a sworn curse-commitment more precisely than a simple "oath."

9. **26:33** — LXX renders *Šiḇ'āh* as *Horkos* (Ὅρκος, "oath/sworn thing") — capturing the oath meaning of the root *šb'* without the "seven" numeric nuance.

10. **26:34–35** — LXX *erithousan* (ἐριθοῦσαν, "contentious/troublesome") for MT *mōrat rûaḥ* ("bitterness of spirit") — loses the specific *rûaḥ* imagery; emphasizes the behavioral quarrelsomeness of the wives rather than the emotional anguish of the parents.

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, no significant Genesis 26 fragments from the Dead Sea Scrolls are extant. The Leningrad Codex (primary) and Codex Vaticanus LXX (secondary) are the principal manuscript witnesses examined for this chapter.
